content.title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline
content.aim: The guideline provides an update to and consolidates new evidence since the 2012 ACCF/AHA/ACP/AATS/PCNA/SCAI/STS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Patients With Stable Ischemic Heart Disease and the corresponding 2014 focused update.
content.methods: A comprehensive literature search was conducted from September 2021 to May 2022, including clinical studies, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses from sources like MEDLINE, EMBASE, Cochrane Library, and others.
content.structure: This guideline provides evidence-based and patient-centered management of patients with chronic coronary disease. It includes recommendations concerning treatment decisions, revascularization, patient monitoring, and special population management. New data has been incorporated where applicable.
content.approvals: Approved by the American College of Cardiology and the American Heart Association Committees in early 2023.

messages.headline: Emphasis on team-based, patient-centered care
messages.details: Considers social determinants of health along with associated costs. Encourages shared decision-making in risk assessment, testing, and treatment.

messages.headline: Nonpharmacologic therapies
messages.details: Including healthy dietary habits and exercise are recommended for all patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD).

messages.headline: Participation in rehabilitation and physical activity
messages.details: Patients with CCD should engage in activities that reduce sitting time and increase aerobic and resistance exercises. Cardiac rehabilitation provides significant cardiovascular benefits.

messages.headline: Use of medications
messages.details: Sodium glucose cotransporter 2 inhibitors and glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists recommended for CCD patients, including those with diabetes.

messages.headline: Beta-blocker therapy
messages.details: Long-term therapy is not recommended unless specific cardiovascular conditions are present.

messages.headline: Statins as first-line therapy
messages.details: For lipid lowering in patients with CCD. Other therapies like ezetimibe and PCSK9 inhibitors can be used in select cases.

messages.headline: Duration of antiplatelet therapy
messages.details: Shorter durations are safe and effective when the bleeding risk is low.

messages.headline: Avoidance of nonprescription supplements
messages.details: Such as fish oil and omega-3 fatty acids is recommended due to lack of benefit in CCD.

messages.headline: Routine testing
messages.details: Not recommended unless there is a change in clinical status.

messages.headline: Use of e-cigarettes
messages.details: Not recommended as first-line therapy for smoking cessation due to lack of long-term safety data.

preamble.background: Since 1980, the American College of Cardiology (ACC) and American Heart Association (AHA) have developed guidelines to improve cardiovascular health.
preamble.INTENT: Guidelines aim to improve the quality of care and align with patient interests globally.
preamble.methodology: The AHA/ACC Joint Committee on Clinical Practice Guidelines reviews and updates guidelines regularly.

genes.context: PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9) inhibitors may be used in select populations for lipid lowering in patients with CCD.

content.Guideline Development: Guidelines are re-evaluated and modified to account for evolving technologies and factors to facilitate information dissemination to healthcare professionals. The guidelines are modular and include a table of recommendations, a brief synopsis, supportive text, and when appropriate, flow diagrams or additional tables.
content.Cost-Value Considerations: There is a recognition of the importance of cost-value considerations in the guidelines when feasible.
content.Evidence Review: Recommendations are based on evidence-based methodologies, including RCTs, registries, and comparative studies. An independent review committee is formed for crucial questions requiring a systematic review.
content.Guideline-Directed Management and Therap: Encompasses clinical evaluation, diagnostic testing, and both pharmacological and procedural treatments limited to those approved for use in the United States.

content.publication_info: This clinical guideline is titled the "2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline." It was conducted from September 24, 2021, to May 2022 and reviewed, among others, by the American Heart Association (AHA) and the American College of Cardiology (ACC).
content.search_databases: Key search words included AHA/ACC Clinical Practice Guidelines, acute coronary syndrome, and others related to cardiovascular diseases.
content.recommendations: The guideline is intended to provide recommendations on the treatment and management of patients with chronic coronary disease, focusing on areas such as lipid management, diabetes management, and revascularization.
content.evaluation_criteria: The levels of value are categorized into high, intermediate, low, and uncertain based on cost-effectiveness.
content.organization: The writing committee included representatives from various organizations such as the AHA and ACC.
content.review: The document was reviewed by official reviewers nominated by several organizations, including ACC and AHA.
content.scope: The guideline aims to provide an update with new evidence and a patient-centered approach for the management of chronic coronary disease.

content.Purpose: The guideline provides recommendations for outpatient care of patients with Chronic Coronary Disease (CCD).
Patient Categories: Patients discharged after admission for ACS event or after coronary revascularization.
Patients with left ventricular systolic dysfunction and known or suspected CAD.
Patients with stable angina symptoms medically managed with or without positive imaging results.
Patients with angina symptoms and evidence of coronary vasospasm or microvascular angina.
Patients diagnosed with CCD based only on screening results.
Guideline Structure: Section 3: Evaluation, Diagnosis, and Risk Stratification.
Key Recommendations: Prioritize therapies based on future risk of CVD-related events.
Improve symptom relief and quality of life.
Recommendation of therapies that improve symptom relief without worsening outcomes.
Collaboration between clinicians in primary care and cardiology specialties.
Consideration for asymptomatic patients with significant coronary artery calcium noted on noncardiac imaging.
content.Unaddressed Areas: Patients needing aggressive lifestyle management and medical therapies to prevent atherosclerosis progression are not covered.
content.Guideline Updates: In 2023, existing guidelines were reviewed and some recommendations were modified for format or adapted substantively.

Class of Recommendation (COR).Definition: Indicates the strength of recommendation, encompassing the estimated magnitude and certainty of benefit in proportion to risk.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 2b (Weak): Benefit ≥ Risk. Actions that may be considered.
Class of Recommendation (COR).Class 3: Harm: Risk > Benefit. Actions that are potentially harmful.

Level of Evidence (LOE).Purpose: Rates the quality of scientific evidence supporting the intervention based on type, quantity, and consistency of data from clinical trials and other sources.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level A: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT or meta-analyses of high-quality RCTs.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level B-R: Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs.
Level of Evidence (LOE).Level C-LD: Limited data. Evidence from nonrandomized or registry studies.

content.Clinical Guideline Overview: The guideline provides information on chronic coronary disease (CCD), which includes both obstructive and nonobstructive coronary artery disease. It discusses the epidemiology and prevalence of CCD in the United States and worldwide, highlighting the substantial burden associated with the disease. The intersection of CCD with other conditions such as atrial fibrillation and chronic kidney disease is addressed, emphasizing the importance of diagnosis, risk stratification, and treatment.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses advancements in diabetes and lipid management therapies, diagnostic, and interventional procedures in patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD). It emphasizes evaluation, diagnosis, and risk stratification, recommending diagnostic evaluations like imaging and stress tests for patients whose symptoms persist despite guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT).

Recommendation 1.Details: For patients with CCD and persistent symptoms or functional capacity changes despite GDMT, imaging with stress tests (PET/SPECT, CMR, echocardiography) is recommended to detect ischemia, assess cardiovascular events risk, and guide decision-making.

Recommendation 2.Details: In patients with CCD and persistent symptoms, invasive coronary angiography (ICA) is recommended to guide therapeutic decision-making to improve symptoms.

Recommendation 3.Details: For those selected for rest/stress nuclear MPI, PET is preferred over SPECT to improve accuracy and reduce unnecessary tests.

Recommendation 4.Details: Exercise treadmill testing can be helpful in symptomatic patients to evaluate consistency with angina, assess symptoms severity, evaluate capacity, and guide management.

Recommendation 5.Details: For those undergoing PET MPI or CMR, assessment of myocardial blood flow reserve can improve diagnostic accuracy and risk stratification.

Recommendation 6.Details: For patients with previous coronary revascularization and persistent symptoms, coronary CT angiography (CCTA) is reasonable to evaluate bypass grafts or stent patency.

content.Synopsis: Clinicians should intensify GDMT and defer testing when appropriate. Imaging should be considered in new-onset or persistent chest pain patients, CCD with frequent or severe stress-induced ischemia, and in those eligible for intervention.

General Information: Several large multicenter registries indicate stress CMR imaging effectively stratifies patients with known CAD.
Abnormal stress CMR images correlate with a nearly 2-fold increased mortality hazard.
Patient prognosis is tied to the extent of stress echocardiography abnormalities.
Exercise treadmill testing aids in assessing CAD-related chest pain.
CCTA is accurate for native CAD and bypass graft patency assessment.
FFR-CT shows high diagnostic sensitivity compared to invasive FFR.

Clinical Studies.Outcome: Greater reduction in stress echocardiographic wall motion score with PCI over placebo.
No difference in primary MACE endpoint between invasive vs conservative care after ~3.3 years.

Diagnostic Tools: SPECT MPI and PET MPI for CAD patients help in risk stratification.
CCTA and FFR-CT provide high accuracy in CAD assessment.

content.Risk Stratification and Relationship to: The guideline recommends that risk stratification for patients with CCD incorporate noninvasive and invasive diagnostics to accurately assess cardiovascular risk. This should include a combination of clinical, demographic, and other relevant data. Regular testing to assess risk and optimize GDMT is stressed for its effectiveness in reducing MACE.
content.Risk Stratification and Prognosis: 1. Risk stratification should use a variety of diagnostic tools to classify risk levels for cardiovascular death or nonfatal MI.
content.Relationship to Treatment: 1. Optimization of GDMT is recommended for patients with CCD. 2. Newly reduced LV systolic function should prompt coronary anatomy assessment and potential revascularization. 3. ICA not routinely recommended in the absence of LV dysfunction or noninvasive test indications.
content.Potential Features Associated with Highe: Includes age, sex, social support, poverty, medical conditions like elevated BMI, previous MI, PCI, CABG, HF, diabetes, and lifestyle factors like smoking.

content.General Information: The 2021 AHA/ACC chest pain guideline recommends optimizing anti-ischemic and preventive therapies to reduce the patient's angina burden and improve clinical outcomes. There is no reduction in MACE with routine cardiovascular revascularization. Striving for improved outcomes involves categorizing patients based on cardiovascular risk. The COURAGE trial, ISCHEMIA trial, and BARI-2D trial found that optimal medical therapy provides similar outcomes to invasive procedures in certain populations. The guidelines emphasize the use of CCTA as an important diagnostic tool in some cases, especially for noninvasive anatomical assessments.

Recommendations.Text: In patients with CCD, clinical follow-up at least annually is recommended to assess for symptoms, change in functional status, adherence to and adequacy of lifestyle and medical interventions, and monitoring for complications of CCD and its treatments.
In patients with CCD, use of a validated CCD-specific patient-reported health status measure may be reasonable to assess symptoms, functional status, and QOL.

content.Synopsis: The ultimate goals for treatment of CCD are to prolong survival and improve QOL. Treatment should target a reduction in cardiac death, nonfatal ischemic events, progression of atherosclerosis, and limitations of CCD while considering patient preferences, potential complications, and costs. Treatment strategies should include a general assessment of the patient’s risk, careful assessment of symptoms of CCD, and evaluation of QOL.

Recommendation-Specific Text.Patient Group: Patients with CCD comprise a heterogeneous group, including those with or without angina, history of coronary revascularization, and previous ACS.
Goals: Assess for new or worsened symptoms, change in functional status, or decline in QOL.
Assess adherence to lifestyle and medical interventions.
Monitor for complications of disease or therapy.

content.Figure Description: Domains to Consider When Seeing a Patient With CCD
Textual Information: Status instruments measure patient's angina impact on QOL for treatment decisions.
Studies show deficiencies in clinician estimation of patient's symptoms.
No studies show improvement in care with routine use of patient-reported measures.
Team-Based Approach recommended to improve outcomes and modify ASCVD risk factors.
content.Recommendation: In patients with CCD, a multidisciplinary team-based approach is recommended to improve health outcomes and facilitate modification of ASCVD risk factors.

content.Synopsis: A patient-centered, team-based approach that focuses on shared decision-making is essential to monitoring and managing patients' CCD symptoms throughout their disease course. These recommendations apply to all aspects of clinical practice for long-term management of CCD. A team-based approach can effectively be applied to nearly all aspects of CCD management and care. Continuous communication among the care team, the patient, and any caregivers is essential to optimize outcomes and meet patient needs.
Patient Care Involvement: Clinician (Physician, Advanced Practice Nurse, Physician Assistant)

General recommendations: RCTs and systematic reviews show that patient-centered, multidisciplinary, team-based approaches improve patient self-efficacy, health-related quality of life (QOL), and ASCVD risk factor management in patients with CCD.
A team-based approach is cost-effective compared with usual care and reduces emergency visits, unplanned health service utilization, and complications in patients with diabetes.
Patient education is defined as delivering information to alter patient health behaviors or improve health status.
Educational interventions improve patient knowledge but have unclear impact on lifestyle or mortality rates.
Education should be individualized, literacy-appropriate, and culturally tailored.
Patient Education Recommendations: Patients with CCD should receive individualized education on symptom management, lifestyle changes, and SDOH risk factors.
Patients with CCD should receive education on medication adherence.

content.summary: The document provides guidelines for shared decision-making in patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD). The guidelines emphasize patient education about risks, benefits, and alternatives to treatment and testing options. It underlines the importance of patient involvement in decision-making to improve outcomes. Specific recommendations include the use of validated decision aids and improving medication adherence through educational interventions.

recommendations.text: In patients with CCD, routine assessment by clinicians and the care team for SDOH is recommended to inform patient-centered treatment decisions and lifestyle change recommendations.

topics: Decision aids can improve patient knowledge, accuracy of risk perception, and agreement between patient values and care choices.
SDOH impacts all stages of CCD management and includes factors such as access to care, economic stability, and social context.
Routine screenings for SDOH are recommended.
Collaborative care, incorporating SDOH, can enhance health outcomes.
impacts: SDOH are key drivers of health disparities in patients with CCD.
Neighborhood disadvantage and socioeconomic factors significantly impact cardiovascular outcomes.

content.Title: Social Determinants of Health and Cardiovascular Care for Patients With CCD
content.Focus: Actionable Steps for Clinicians and Care Teams

Categories.Title: Health Care System
Points: Lack of health insurance and/or underinsured
Refer for insurance subsidies for coverage of services and medications to support adherence as necessary
Expand health care services options (eg, telehealth, extended hours, patient portals) to foster longitudinal follow-up and cardiac rehabilitation participation

Categories.Title: Education/Health Literacy
Points: Assess health literacy to ensure patient understanding of treatment recommendations
Provide patient education materials at appropriate education level

Categories.Title: Economic Stability
Points: Refer to social services for local employment/financial services options
Provide community-based resources and subsidies for affordable food options to support healthy diet

Categories.Title: Physical Environment
Points: Refer to social services for viable housing options
Provide community-based resources for affordable and safe recreational facilities and municipal parks to promote physical activity
Provide telehealth and digital health options (mobile devices) to support healthy lifestyle

Categories.Title: Gender Considerations and/or Sexual Orientation
Points: Tailor care to meet patient personal preferences
Consider sex differences in treatment responses

Categories.Title: Culture and Language
Points: Review sociocultural considerations affecting health (eg, faith/spirituality, body image, age)

Categories.Title: Systemic Racism
Points: Constrained access to health, financial, and physical environmental resources
Apply equity-focused lens to care to foster enhanced patient-provider communication

Categories.Title: Social Support
Points: Refer as needed to mental health professionals
Assess social capital/network to support health behaviors and health care participation

content.Title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline

Nutrition.Recommendation: Emphasize vegetables, fruits, legumes, nuts, whole grains, and lean protein to reduce CVD events.
Reduce calories from saturated fat, replace with mono/polyunsaturated fat, complex carbs, dietary fiber.
Minimize sodium intake and processed meats to be beneficial.
Limit refined carbs and sugar-sweetened beverages.
Intake of trans fat should be avoided due to association with increased mortality rates.

Nutrition Supplements.Recommendation: Nonprescription use of supplements like omega-3 fatty acids, vitamins C, D, E, beta-carotene, and calcium is not beneficial for reducing acute CVD events.

content.Synopsis: Dietary changes with GDMT help reduce risk of CVD events, supporting weight loss and improving cardiometabolic health. Nonprescription nutrition or dietary supplements lack evidence for effectiveness.

choose_these: Monounsaturated fat (<20% of daily calories; e.g., olive oil)
Polyunsaturated fat (<10% of daily calories; e.g., salmon)
instead_of_these: Sugar-sweetened beverages (e.g., sugar-added soft drinks, fruit drinks)

clinical_guidelines: Implementation of healthy plant-based and Mediterranean-based diets includes reducing saturated fat and optimizing caloric intake.
Higher dietary fiber intake is associated with improvement in CVD risk factors.
The AHA recommends lowering saturated fatty acids to <6% of total caloric intake.
Dietary sodium reduction to <2,300 mg/d may improve BP and reduce CVD event risk.
Minimizing intake of simple carbohydrates and refined grains supports a healthier cardiometabolic profile.
Sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is associated with increased risk of CVD events.

content.document_title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline (selected page)

points: Multiple healthy dietary components, including reduction in dietary sodium, sugar-sweetened beverages, and saturated fat reduce all-cause death among secondary prevention cohorts.
Recommendations are unavailable for artificial sweeteners due to limited data in populations with CCD.

Consumption of trans fats has been associated with an increased risk of CVD events, including CVD mortality rate, and all-cause death in primary prevention populations and among individuals with CCD; association primarily attributed to industrially processed hydrogenated vegetable oils and ruminant trans fats.

In patients at high risk for CCD, nonprescription dietary omega-3 fatty acid supplements (eg, capsules, oil, soft gels) do not reduce CVD events or all-cause death; a Cochrane meta-analysis including 86 RCTs showed “little or no effect.”
Insufficient evidence that vitamin D supplementation reduces CVD events; meta-analysis of RCTs showed vitamin D did not lower risk of MACE.
Antioxidant therapy (vitamin C, beta-carotene, multivitamins) is not associated with decreased CVD event risk or CVD mortality rate.

Insufficient data to support calcium supplementation (elemental calcium ≥500 mg/d) for CVD event reduction; a meta-analysis of double-blind RCTs showed an increased risk of CVD and CHD events with calcium supplementation versus placebo.

sections_extracted.section_heading: Recommendations for Mental Health Conditions (table summary)

table.Recommendation: In patients with CCD, targeted discussions and screening for mental health is reasonable for clinicians to assess and to refer for additional mental health evaluation and management.
In patients with CCD, treatment for mental health conditions with either pharmacologic or non-pharmacologic therapies, or both, is reasonable to improve cardiovascular outcomes.

key_points: Mental health has a major role in overall cardiovascular health and well-being in patients with CCD.
Mental health defined as a state of well-being in which an individual realizes abilities, copes with normal stresses, works productively, and contributes to community.
Estimated 20% to 40% of patients with CCD have concomitant mental health conditions such as depression and anxiety.
Negative psychological states (eg, general distress) associated with MACE in men and women with CCD.
Psychopharmacologic and psychotherapeutic treatments may reduce recurrent cardiovascular events and mortality in patients with CCD.
Screening for mental health disorders is seldom addressed in the clinical setting despite being a modifiable prognostic risk factor.

points: Mental health factors (including depression, anxiety, anger, general distress, and type D personality) are common in diverse populations with CCD.

Psychological stressors (eg, financial hardship, social isolation, discrimination) can have deleterious effects and are linked to diminished quality of life, atherosclerotic disease progression, and negative impacts on medication and cardiac rehabilitation adherence, diet, physical activity, sleep, and other risk factors.

content.clinical_guideline: This document discusses the use of depression screening among patients with recent Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS) and its association with cardiovascular outcomes. Positive screening for depression is linked to an increased risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE). The use of antidepressants shows varying results on cardiovascular outcomes, and the document suggests considering both pharmacologic and psychotherapeutic interventions for patients with Chronic Coronary Disease (CCD) and depression. Mindfulness and cognitive-behavioral therapy are recommended for improving various psychological and cardiovascular factors.

screening_tools.name: Patient Health Questionnaire-2
screening_tools.purpose: To assess psychological distress and depression symptoms.

treatment_recommendations: Combination pharmacologic and psychotherapeutic treatments are suggested.
Mindfulness and psychotherapy interventions are advised to improve depression and related health factors.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with CCD, tobacco use should be assessed at every health care visit to facilitate identification of those who may benefit from behavioral or pharmacologic interventions.
Patients with CCD who regularly smoke tobacco should be advised to quit at every visit.

In patients with CCD who regularly smoke tobacco, behavioral interventions are recommended to maximize cessation rates in combination with pharmacotherapy, including bupropion, varenicline, or combination long- and short-acting nicotine replacement therapy (NRT).
In patients with CCD who regularly smoke tobacco, varenicline may be considered versus bupropion or NRT to increase cessation rates.

In patients with CCD who regularly smoke tobacco, the short-term use of nicotine-containing e-cigarettes may be considered to aid smoking cessation, although the risk of sustained use and unknown long-term safety may outweigh the benefits.
Patients with CCD should avoid secondhand smoke exposure to reduce risk of cardiovascular events.

content.synopsis: Tobacco smoke exposure, particularly cigarette smoking, is a leading cause of CVD and cardiovascular events in patients with CCD. Smoking cessation is associated with decreased risk of death and MI. Pharmacotherapy and behavioral therapy can increase cessation success. E-cigarettes may increase CHD risk, albeit lower than cigarettes.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Most persons who smoke report they want to quit smoking.
Systematic assessment of tobacco and e-cigarette use facilitates smoking cessation.
Routine screening for smoking status increases clinician intervention rates.
Electronic health record-based interventions can support smoking cessation programs.
Smoking cessation short-term effects: decreased heart rate, BP, and improved endothelial function.

resources.name: Telephone-based: Quitline
resources.description: Counseling by telephone from a trained tobacco coach who offers support via a series of scheduled telephone calls before and after a smoker's quit date. Patients can self-refer or be referred by clinicians. Quitline services vary by state and may include text messaging, web coaching support, and free samples of nicotine replacement therapy.

resources.name: Web-based: American Lung Association Freedom From Smoking
resources.description: Program helps build a customized quit plan with support options including group clinics, a self-help guide, and web-based interactive program.

resources.name: Web-based: National Cancer Institute
resources.description: Provides information about quitting and resources for personalization. Specific sites available for women, teens, veterans, and those over 60.

resources.name: Web-based: Asian Smokers' Quitline
resources.description: Created for support tobacco cessation for persons who speak these languages across the US. Participants may receive starter kits.

resources.name: Web-based: BecomeAnEX
resources.description: Created by the Truth Initiative for cessation of smoking, vaping, or use of smokeless tobacco. Supports customized quit plans and provides resources for teens and young adults.

key_points: Clinician advice increases quit rates.
Behavioral therapy effective for cessation.
NRT and varenicline increase success rates.

content.summary: The guideline discusses the efficacy of smoking cessation methods in patients with CVD, favoring varenicline over bupropion or NRT. It also highlights the lack of data for the efficacy of e-cigarettes and smokeless tobacco for smoking cessation and suggests more research is needed. Nicotine e-cigarettes appear more effective than NRT for cessation but have associated risks like long-term dependence and adverse effects on cardiovascular health. The document advises against recommending e-cigarettes as first-line therapy. Secondhand smoke is also harmful, emphasizing policies to reduce exposure. Additionally, recommendations for alcohol consumption in CCD patients suggest limited intake and not using alcohol for cardiovascular protection. Various substances have adverse interactions with cardiovascular therapies.

routine_counseling.recommendation: Patients with CCD should be routinely asked and counseled about substance use to reduce ASCVD events.

alcohol_consumption.recommendation: In patients with CCD who consume alcohol, it is reasonable to limit alcohol intake to reduce cardiovascular and all-cause death.

alcohol_for_cardio_protection.recommendation: Patients with CCD should not be advised to consume alcohol for the purpose of cardiovascular protection.

content.alcohol_relationship: No RCTs support moderate alcohol consumption to reduce cardiovascular risk, with studies suggesting no safe level of alcohol use.
content.substance_use: Substance use, including cannabis, amphetamines, opioids, marijuana, and alcohol, is a significant contributor to cardiovascular disease risk.

cardiovascular_observations.alcohol: A J-shaped relationship exists between alcohol consumption and death, with a lower incidence of cardiovascular and all-cause death at 5-25 g/d.
cardiovascular_observations.cocaine_amphetamines: Associated with stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system, increased myocardial oxygen demand, and cocaine-associated chest pain.
cardiovascular_observations.opioids: Possibly associated with MI, high potential for dependence, and drug-drug interactions.
cardiovascular_observations.marijuana: Involves sympathetic nervous stimulation, platelet activation, endothelial dysfunction, and carbon monoxide toxicity.

content.study_findings: In patients presenting with an MI from 2000 to 2016, 10% were under 50 years old, with recreational drug use associated with premature CVD.
content.recommendation: Substance use significantly contributes to CVD risk. Long-term opioid use for patients with CCD and chronic pain should be avoided.

Recommendations.Text: In patients with CCD, it is reasonable to individualize resumption of sexual activity based on type of sexual activity, exercise capacity, and postprocedural healing.
In patients with CCD, cardiac rehabilitation and regular exercise can be useful to reduce the risk of cardiovascular complications with sexual activity.
In patients with CCD, phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors should not be used concomitantly with nitrate medications because of risk for severe hypotension.

content.Synopsis: Sexual health is important to QOL. Sexual activity is moderate physical activity around 3 to 5 metabolic equivalents. Patients with CCD should reach this level during exercise testing without symptoms. Sexual activity is associated with 1% of all MIs. Men and women with CCD and risk factors have a high prevalence of sexual dysfunction. Recent MI and CABG may affect sexual function. Patients with CCD who are medically compensated should be advised on safe sexual activity levels.

genes.context: PRIME (Prospective Epidemiological Study of Myocardial Infarction) found that binge drinking (>50 g at least once a week) was associated with a higher risk of coronary events (hazard ratio, 2.03 [95% CI, 1.41-2.94]) compared with regular drinking.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with CCD, high-intensity statin therapy is recommended with the aim of achieving a ≥50% reduction in LDL-C levels to reduce the risk of MACE.

In patients in whom high-intensity statin therapy is contraindicated or not tolerated, moderate-intensity statin therapy is recommended with the aim of achieving a 30% to 49% reduction in LDL-C levels to reduce the risk of MACE.

In patients with CCD, adherence to changes in lifestyle and effects of lipid-lowering medication should be assessed by measurement of fasting lipids in 4 to 12 weeks after statin initiation or dose adjustment and then every 3 to 12 months thereafter based on need to assess response or adherence to therapy.
In patients with CCD, the use of generic formulations of maximally tolerated statin therapy is projected to be cost saving.

In patients with CCD who are judged to be at very high risk and on maximally tolerated statin therapy with an LDL-C level ≥70 mg/dL (≥1.8 mmol/L), ezetimibe can be beneficial to further reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with CCD, addition of generic ezetimibe to maximally tolerated statin therapy in appropriately selected patients is projected to be of high economic value at US prices.

In patients with CCD who are judged to be at very high risk and who have an LDL-C level ≥70 mg/dL (≥1.8 mmol/L), or a non-high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C) level ≥100 mg/dL (≥2.6 mmol/L), on maximally tolerated statin and ezetimibe, a PCSK9 monoclonal antibody can be beneficial to further reduce the risk of MACE.
In patients with CCD who are very high risk, the use of PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies is projected to be of uncertain economic value at US prices.

In patients with CCD on maximally tolerated statin therapy with an LDL-C level <100 mg/dL (<2.6 mmol/L) and a persistent fasting triglyceride level of 150 to 499 mg/dL (1.7-5.6 mmol/L) after addressing secondary causes, icosapent ethyl may be considered to further reduce the risk of MACE and cardiovascular death.

In patients with CCD who are not at very high risk and on maximally tolerated statin therapy with an LDL-C level ≥70 mg/dL (≥1.8 mmol/L), it may be reasonable to add ezetimibe to further reduce the risk of MACE.

In patients with CCD on maximally tolerated statin therapy who have an LDL-C level ≥70 mg/dL (≥1.8 mmol/L), and in whom ezetimibe and PCSK9 monoclonal antibody are deemed insufficient or not tolerated, it may be reasonable to add bempedoic acid or inclisiran.
In patients with CCD receiving statin therapy, adding niacin or fenofibrate or dietary supplements containing omega-3 fatty acids, are not beneficial in reducing cardiovascular risk.

genes.context: A PCSK9 monoclonal antibody can be beneficial to further reduce the risk of MACE. In patients with CCD who are very high risk, the use of PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies is projected to be of uncertain economic value at US prices.

LDL-C lowering therapies: High-intensity statin therapy is recommended as the preferred initial approach to reduce LDL-C by ≥50% and reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality rates.
Despite maximally tolerated statin therapy, residual cardiovascular risk persists, especially among patients with CCD and additional high-risk clinical factors.

Several nonstatins do not provide benefit when added to statin therapy: these include ezetimibe, PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies, and icosapent ethyl, which further reduce cardiovascular risk when added to background statin therapy.
Clinicians should prioritize the use of ezetimibe and PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies when additional LDL-C lowering is necessary on patients on maximally tolerated statin therapy.
Lipid monitoring is essential to assess individual response to lipid-lowering therapy and monitor adherence and persistence with therapy over time.
Statin intolerance: Statin intolerance may be complete or partial and requires a trial of at least 2 statins with one at the lowest approved daily dose.
Moderate-intensity statin should be used in patients unable to tolerate a high-intensity statin.
Additional strategies may involve alternative statin regimens or alternative dosing.
Recommendations: The CTT (Cholesterol Treatment Trialists) meta-analysis of RCTs showed that lowering LDL-C with high-intensity statins reduces major vascular events by 15%.
Repeating lipid profile measurements every 3 to 12 months is associated with increased adherence to therapy and identification of patients requiring further intensification of treatment.
Economic value of therapies depends on outcomes such as the number of cardiovascular events averted or quality-adjusted life years gained.

genes.context: PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies are mentioned as agents that can improve outcomes when added to statin therapy to further reduce cardiovascular risk, particularly when additional LDL-C lowering is necessary.

content.Definition of Very High-Risk: History of multiple major ASCVD events or one major ASCVD event and multiple high-risk conditions.
Major ASCVD Events: Recent acute coronary syndrome (within the past 12 months)
content.Cost-Effectiveness: Ezetimibe is cost-effective especially in high-risk patients; PCSK9 inhibitors were also evaluated.
content.Outcomes: Significant ASCVD risk reduction observed, with improvements in health outcomes noted in different setups.

genes.context: The FOURIER trial evaluated evolocumab among those with established ASCVD. The cost-effectiveness of adding generic ezetimibe to maximally tolerated statin therapy is sensitive to the assumption regarding the effect of ezetimibe on cardiovascular and all-cause death.

content.table: High-, Moderate-, and Low-Intensity Statin Therapy
content.LDL_C_lowering: Percent LDL-C reductions with the primary statin medications were estimated using the median reduction in LDL-C from the VOYAGER database.

PCSK9_monoclonal_antibodies.Efficacy: Considered for patients with CCD who are at low to moderate risk of adverse cardiovascular events, valuable for those with higher baseline risk of CVD events.
PCSK9_monoclonal_antibodies.Cost: The cost has declined by 60% since their initial market entry, improving cost-effectiveness.

content.REDUCE_IT_Trial: Randomized patients with established ASCVD or diabetes plus additional risk factors to either 4 g/day of icosapent ethyl (purified EPA only) or mineral oil placebo. Showed reduction in MACE by 25% and cardiovascular death by 20%.
content.RESPECT_Trial: Showed a borderline significant reduction in MACE with icosapent ethyl 1800 mg/day in Japanese participants.
content.STRENGTH_Trial: No benefit with a 4 g/day carboxylic acid formulation of omega-3 fatty acids observed, neither harm nor benefit compared to placebo.

genes.context: Clinicians bypassing the addition of ezetimibe before adding a PCSK9 monoclonal antibody should recognize this may not be cost effective. PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies are of low economic value in patients at low to moderate risk of adverse cardiovascular events.

content.Clinical Guideline: Lipid Management in Patients with Chronic Coronary Disease (CCD)

Not at Very High Risk: High-intensity statin recommended with goal of LDL-C reduction ≥50%.
If high-intensity statin not tolerated, use moderate-intensity statin.
Consider icosapent ethyl if triglyceride levels are 150–499 mg/dL.
Very High Risk: Consider adding ezetimibe if LDL-C ≥70 mg/dL.
Consider adding PCSK9 monoclonal antibody if LDL-C lowering is inadequate with statin and ezetimibe.

content.Lifestyle Recommendations: Adherence to a healthy lifestyle is emphasized for all patients.

genes.context: PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies may be considered to further reduce LDL-C levels when ezetimibe and statins are insufficient.

content.title: Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline
content.description: Guidelines on managing chronic coronary disease, focusing on lipid management and lifestyle changes.
content_summary: Bempedoic acid is an ATP-citrate lyase inhibitor that reduces LDL-C levels by 15% to 25% depending on statin background. It is associated with fewer muscle-related side effects.

Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA that targets PCSK9 mRNA, reducing LDL-C levels by approximately 50%. It is administered as a single subcutaneous dose followed by a dose at 3 months, then every 6 months.
Considerations for dietary supplements containing omega-3 fatty acids and their cardiovascular benefits were addressed.
recommendations: Bempedoic acid can be combined with ezetimibe for additional cholesterol reduction.
Inclisiran administration should be performed by a trained health professional.
Low-dose omega-3 fatty acid supplementation does not reduce MACE in patients with coronary heart disease.

nonpharmacologic_management.goal: Ideal body weight aim for ≥1 kg reduction
nonpharmacologic_management.impact_on_sbp: Approximately −5 mm Hg in hypertensive patients

nonpharmacologic_management.goal: Fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and low-fat dairy
nonpharmacologic_management.impact_on_sbp: Approximately −11 mm Hg in hypertensive patients
Approximately −5/6 mm Hg in hypertensive patients

genes.context: Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA directed to break down PCSK9 mRNA, resulting in reduced synthesis of PCSK9. Inclisiran reduces LDL-C levels by approximately 50% and is administered as a single subcutaneous dose, with a second dose at 3 months, then every 6 months.

content.Synopsis: Hypertension is identified as a significant risk factor for cardiovascular disease (CVD) and is prevalent among individuals with chronic coronary disease (CCD). It's noted that over 60% of such individuals are affected. Treatment includes lifestyle modifications and medication therapies, which help control hypertension and reduce subsequent risks. Recommendations are provided for those with CCD who have hypertension and those who are normotensive, focusing on additional lifestyle modifications and blood pressure management.

Recommendations for Blood Pressure Manag.1: For individuals with CCD, nonpharmacologic strategies are recommended as first-line therapy to manage BP for those with elevated BP (120-129/<80 mm Hg).
Recommendations for Blood Pressure Manag.2: In adults with CCD and hypertension, a BP target of <130/<80 mm Hg is recommended to reduce CVD events and all-cause death.
Recommendations for Blood Pressure Manag.3: In adults with CCD and significant hypertension (systolic BP ≥130 and/or diastolic BP ≥80 mm Hg), nonpharmacological strategies and various medications are recommended as first-line therapy for compelling indications. Other medications may be added as needed for BP control.

content.Title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline
content.Summary: The document provides guidelines for the management of chronic coronary disease (CCD), with a focus on the use of SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists in patients with type 2 diabetes and heart failure. It discusses the cardiovascular benefits and cost-effectiveness of these medications.

Patients with CCD and Type 2 Diabetes: Use of SGLT2 inhibitors or GLP-1 receptor agonists is recommended to reduce risk of major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE).
Addition of a GLP-1 receptor agonist or an SGLT2 inhibitor is cost-effective and recommended.
Patients with CCD and Heart Failure (LVE: SGLT2 inhibitors recommended to reduce risk of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalization.
Addition of an SGLT2 inhibitor is considered of intermediate value.
SGLT2 inhibitor use can decrease heart failure hospitalizations and improve quality of life.
Addition of an SGLT2 inhibitor is of uncertain value.

Cost-Value Statements: Use of these medications offers high to intermediate value depending on specific conditions.
content.Synopsis: The guidelines describe cardiovascular risk reduction strategies, emphasizing lifestyle modifications, GDMT, and other management like weight, glucose, and lipid control. Recommendations include maintaining specific targets for blood pressure, glucose control, and overall cardiovascular health.

content.Guideline Overview: The document discusses recommendations for managing chronic coronary disease (CCD) using SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists. It highlights their benefits in reducing cardiovascular events, hospitalization, and improving quality of life, especially in patients with type 2 diabetes and heart failure.

Clinical Recommendations.Description: SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 receptor agonists significantly reduce major adverse cardiovascular events (MACE), weight loss, and progression of kidney disease in patients with CCD and type 2 diabetes.
Benefits: Reduction in hospitalizations for heart failure
Improved functional capacity and quality of life
Clinical Recommendations.Economic Evaluation: Cost-effectiveness analyses show that both SGLT2 inhibitors and GLP-1 are cost-saving or cost-effective over a lifetime horizon.

Clinical Trials.Focus: Reduction of cardiovascular death or HF hospitalization in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
Clinical Trials.Results: SGLT2 inhibitors were shown to improve outcomes and are projected to be cost-effective.

Clinical Trials.Focus: Patients with heart failure and preserved ejection fraction.
Clinical Trials.Results: SGLT2 inhibitors improved functional capacity and reduced risk of hospitalizations.

Patient Populations: Patients with type 2 diabetes and CCD
Patients with heart failure (both reduced and preserved ejection fraction)
Caution needed in patients with type 1 diabetes or advanced CKD

content.generalInformation: The guideline discusses chronic coronary disease (CCD) management, focusing on heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, the use of SGLT2 inhibitors, and weight management. SGLT2 inhibitors are noted for potential effectiveness in reducing cardiovascular mortality among patients with CCD. Recommendations include regular BMI assessment for patients with CCD, counseling for overweight patients, and consideration of GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight reduction. Warnings against the use of sympathomimetic weight loss drugs are also mentioned.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.1: Patients with CCD should undergo routine measurement of BMI with or without waist circumference for initial evaluation and as a guide to efficacy of weight loss intervention.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.2: Lifestyle modification with associated weight loss improves obesity-related CCD comorbidities. A weight loss of 5% to 7% of body weight is typically beneficial.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.3: Candidates for weight-loss drug therapy include individuals with a BMI ≥30 kg/m² or a BMI of 27 to 29.9 kg/m² with weight-related comorbidities who have not met weight-loss goals.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.4: Patients with CCD and severe obesity (BMI ≥40 kg/m² or BMI 35-39.9 kg/m² with a weight-related comorbidity) may benefit from a bariatric procedure.

study_outcomes: significant reductions in MACE among those undergoing surgery

sympathomimetic_drugs.FDA_advisory: removed sibutramine from the US market in 2010

cardiac_rehabilitation.recommendation: All patients with CCD and appropriate indications should be referred to a cardiac rehabilitation program.

CR_delivery_models.description: alternative for patients who cannot attend facility-based CR

recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Patients with recent MI, PCI, or CABG who participate in CR have better outcomes.

content.Overview: The clinical guideline emphasizes the importance of physical activity in individuals with chronic coronary disease (CCD) for improving functional capacity, quality of life, and reducing hospital admission and mortality rates. Recommendations focus on moderate-to-vigorous aerobic and resistance training.

Recommendations.Details: For patients with CCD who do not have contraindications, a regimen of ≥150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic activities or ≥75 minutes per week of higher-intensity aerobic activities is recommended.
Resistance training exercises are recommended on ≥2 days per week to improve muscle strength, functional capacity, and cardiovascular risk factor control.

Lower-intensity lifestyle activities, like walking breaks at work, are reasonable to reduce sedentary behavior and cardiovascular risk, especially in individuals with low levels of habitual leisure-time physical activity.

content.Benefits: Physical activity, including aerobic and resistance training, is associated with improved cardiovascular outcomes, metabolic health, and reduced mortality. The combination of aerobic exercise and resistance training is effective for those with CCD.
content.Contraindications: Exercise is contraindicated in patients with severe, life-threatening, and unstable conditions, including unstable angina, high-risk cardiovascular conditions, or severe orthopedic limitations.

recommendations.description: In patients with CCD, minimization of exposure to ambient air pollution is reasonable to reduce the risk of cardiovascular events.
In patients with CCD, minimization of climate-related exposures (eg, extreme temperatures, wildfire smoke) may be reasonable to reduce the risk of cardiovascular events.

content.synopsis: Adverse environmental exposures such as air pollution, extremes of ambient temperature, and excess noise should be assessed in patients with CCD. Exposure to air pollution is associated with increased cardiovascular risk. Extreme temperatures have been linked with adverse cardiovascular events. High-efficiency air purifiers or N95 filters may improve outcomes. Ground-level ozone also contributes to cardiovascular risk. Individual risk assessments and adaptive strategies are important, especially for at-risk populations.

content.Overview: The guideline provides recommendations for antiplatelet therapy and oral anticoagulants in patients with Chronic Coronary Disease (CCD).

Items.Recommendation: Low-dose aspirin (81 mg) is recommended to reduce atherosclerotic events for patients with CCD and no indication for oral anticoagulant therapy.

In patients with CCD treated with PCI, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) consisting of aspirin and clopidogrel for 6 months post-PCI followed by single antiplatelet therapy (SAPT) is indicated to reduce MACE and bleeding events.

In select patients with CCD treated with PCI and a drug-eluting stent (DES), a 3-month course of DAPT followed by P2Y12 inhibitor monotherapy for at least 12 months is reasonable to reduce bleeding risk.
For patients with CCD and a previous MI, extended DAPT beyond 12 months for up to 3 years may be reasonable to reduce MACE.
In patients with CCD and a history of MI without a history of stroke, TIA, or ICH, vorapaxar may be added to aspirin therapy to reduce MACE.
In patients with CCD, the use of DAPT after CABG may reduce the incidence of saphenous vein graft occlusion.
In patients with CCD without recent ACS or PCI-related indication for DAPT, the addition of clopidogrel to aspirin is not useful.
In patients with CCD and previous stroke, TIA, or ICH, vorapaxar should not be added to DAPT due to increased bleeding risk.
In patients with CCD and previous stroke, TIA, or ICH, prasugrel should not be used due to risk of significant or fatal bleeding.
In patients with CCD, chronic NSAIDs should not be used due to increased cardiovascular and bleeding complications.

Recommendations.Category: Antiplatelet Therapy With Direct Oral Anticoagulant (DOAC)

Items.Recommendation: In patients who have undergone elective PCI and require oral anticoagulant therapy, DAPT for 1 to 4 weeks followed by clopidogrel for 6 months should be administered in addition to DOAC.

For those who have undergone PCI and require oral anticoagulant therapy, continuing aspirin in addition to clopidogrel for up to 1 month is reasonable if the patient has a high thrombotic risk and low bleeding risk.

content.general_summary: The guideline focuses on antiplatelet therapy in patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD), discussing the use of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT), direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs), and low-dose aspirin for ischemic event prevention and bleeding risk management. It highlights the use of risk scores for assessing bleeding risks and provides specific recommendations, such as considering aspirin for secondary ASCVD prevention and reducing the duration of DAPT to minimize mortality.

content.guideline: The guideline provides recommendations on the duration of antiplatelet therapy for patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD) following acute coronary syndrome (ACS) and percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI). Different recommendations are provided based on the bleeding and ischemic risk and whether the patient is with or without oral anticoagulant (OAC).
content.study_findings: Data supporting use of DAPT for 6 months after PCI shows lower rates of major bleeding compared to 1-year DAPT. Various trials studied the continuation and efficacy, including ISAR-SAFE, HOST-Exam, and SMART CHOICE.
content.benefits: Clopidogrel showed benefits over aspirin in an East Asian population with lower rates of thrombotic events.

Therapy After Stent Implantation.reduced bleeding complications without i: Meta-analysis showed that short-term DAPT followed by P2Y12 inhibitor monotherapy reduced risk of major bleeding by 40% without increasing MACE. Longer duration DAPT may be needed for high thrombic risk patients.

High-risk Patients.33,435 patients studied, 60% treated wit: Extended DAPT reduced MACE but increased bleeding risk. Analysis of 2016 ACC/AHA guidelines indicated prolonged DAPT reduced thrombosism but increased major hemorrhage in patients with DES.

Vorapaxar.effect: Reduced cardiovascular death, MI, or stroke but increased moderate or severe bleeding in high-risk heart disease patients.

aspirin_clopidogrel.context: not more effective than aspirin alone in reducing the rate of MI, stroke, or death from cardiovascular causes

TRA2P-TIMI_50_trial.context: safety monitoring board recommended discontinuation of study treatment in patients with history of stroke because of the risk of intracranial hemorrhage

vorapaxar.context: FDA approved for use in patients with previous MI for the reduction in cardiovascular death, MI, stroke, or recurrent ischemia; contraindicated with history of stroke, TIA, or intracranial hemorrhage

platelet_inhibition.context: prasugrel increases risk of intracranial hemorrhage and should not be used in patients with active pathological bleeding or history of TIA or stroke

vascular_events.context: major vascular events were increased by a third by coxib compared to placebo

nonsteroidal_anti-inflammatory_drugs.context: high-dose nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory regimens include diclofenac, ibuprofen, and naproxen

DOAC_with_P2Y12_inhibitor.context: associated with less bleeding compared with vitamin K agonists and DAPT

content.stent thrombosis: Low occurrence in <1% over 6 months; lower with aspirin or apixaban compared to WKA.
content.triple therapy: Recommended after PCI for high thrombosis risk; aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor, and oral anticoagulant up to 30 days advised.
content.REDUAL PCI trial: Compared dabigatran dual therapy with warfarin triple therapy; lower major bleeding with dabigatran.
content.AFIRE trial: Rivaroxaban monotherapy noninferior to combination therapy for AF and stable CAD patients.
content.COMAPASS trial: Evaluated low-dose rivaroxaban in reducing CVD events in stable ASCVD patients; terminated early due to efficacy.
content.increased bleeding: Common side effect of DAPT, can cause bleeding; aspirin increases gastrointestinal ulcer risk.

content.Title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline

Recommendations.Text: In patients with CCD and LVEF ≤40% with or without previous MI, the use of beta-blocker therapy is recommended to reduce the risk of future MACE, including cardiovascular death.
In patients with CCD and LVEF <50%, the use of sustained release metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, or bisoprolol with titration to target doses is recommended in preference to other beta blockers.

In patients with CCD who were initiated on beta-blocker therapy for previous MI without a history of or current LVEF ≤50%, angina, arrhythmias, or uncontrolled hypertension, it may be reasonable to reassess the indication for long-term (>1 year) use of beta-blocker therapy for reducing MACE.
In patients with CCD without previous MI or LVEF ≤50%, the use of beta-blocker therapy is not beneficial in reducing MACE, in the absence of another primary indication for beta-blocker therapy.

content.Synopsis: Beta blockers exhibit clinical effects by decreasing myocardial oxygen demand, improving ischemic threshold, and impeding maladaptive LV remodeling. Patients with CCD comprise patients with or without previous MI, LV systolic dysfunction, or both.

Study Data.Findings: Use of PPIs is associated with a reduced risk of gastrointestinal bleeding in patients treated with DAPT after PCI. No significant differences were observed in the incidence of MACE, MI, and all-cause death in patients with CAD on DAPT and PPIs.

content.summary: The clinical guideline discusses the use of beta-blocker therapy in patients with ischemic heart disease, focusing on those with a previous MI (myocardial infarction) and preserved LV (left ventricular) systolic function. The recommendation for long-term use of beta-blockers in the absence of LV systolic dysfunction has been challenged, and various studies showed mixed results. The guideline also covers ongoing clinical trials investigating the continued use of beta-blockers post-MI. Additionally, recommendations for the use of Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone inhibitors are provided, highlighting their efficacy in patients with hypertension, diabetes, reduced LV function, or CKD (chronic kidney disease).
content.beta-blocker therapy: Use in post-MI patients with preserved LV systolic function has mixed results, requiring further studies to ascertain their need and benefit.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.RCTs: RAASi improved symptoms, reduced hospitalizations, prolonged survival among high-risk patients with CCD. It reduced moderate albuminuria and end-stage renal disease.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.HOPE trial: High-risk patients with CCD and preserved LVEF assigned to ramipril had significant reductions in cardiovascular outcomes.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.EUROPA trial: Perindopril reduced events in a lower risk population.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.PEACE trial: Trandolapril was not effective in patients with CCD and normal LVEF but had benefits in those with reduced renal function.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.Colchicine: Rec for secondary prevention in CCD patients to reduce recurrent ASCVD events. Study showed a 47% reduction in patient costs and increased QALYs with colchicine.

Colchicine.Mechanism: Exhibits anti-inflammatory properties by altering inflammatory cell-mediated chemotaxis and by inhibiting microtubule polymerization. Reduces adhesion molecules and has an effect on cytokine production.
Colchicine.Safety: Metabolized by cytochrome P450 3A4 and p-glycoprotein, prone to drug-drug interactions. There is a need for a highly individualized approach.

colchicine.comparison: Patients in the colchicine group had a death rate from noncardiovascular causes of 6.8% compared to 9.6% in the placebo group.
colchicine.findings: The study showed a trend toward an increased risk of death from noncardiovascular causes in the colchicine group.
colchicine.trial: The COLCOT trial evaluated the efficacy of colchicine in secondary prevention in patients with ACS.
colchicine.result: 5.5% of patients in the colchicine group required coronary revascularization compared to 7.1% in the placebo group.

recommendations: An annual influenza vaccination is recommended for patients with CCD to reduce cardiovascular morbidity, cardiovascular death, and all-cause death.
COVID-19 vaccination is recommended for patients with CCD to reduce COVID-19 complications.
Pneumococcal vaccine is recommended for patients with CCD to reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality and all-cause death.

infections.role: Infections like influenza, pneumococcal pneumonia, and COVID-19 contribute to MACE and all-cause death, especially in patients with CCD.
infections.mechanisms: Infections may increase cardiovascular events through pro-inflammatory mediators, stimulation of the sympathetic system, and coagulation cascade activation.

content.clinical_guideline: This document discusses medical antianiginal therapy for patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD). Key recommendations include the use of beta blockers, calcium channel blockers (CCB), and nitrates for relief of angina. Ranolazine and ivabradine are mentioned as additional options, with ivabradine noted as potentially harmful in certain cases. The guideline emphasizes maximizing relief of symptoms without exacerbating comorbidities.
content.vaccination: A study on the pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine and influenza vaccine indicated reduced mortality and cardiac events in patients hospitalized with ischemic heart disease.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with CCD, refractory angina, and no other treatment options, enhanced external counter-pulsation may be considered for relief of symptoms.

study_findings: Beta blockers increase the percentage of patients free of exertional angina, reduce anginal attacks and nitroglycerin consumption.
There is no difference in the rate of death or MI between calcium channel blockers and beta blockers.
CBs effectively relieve angina, decrease nitroglycerin consumption, and increase exercise duration.
Long-acting nitrates decrease angina and improve exercise duration.
Non-dihydropyridine CCBs can worsen LV function in patients with CCD.
Combination treatment with another antianginal agent improves symptoms in patients with CCD and refractory angina.
Two studies found that ranolazine improved angina outcomes.
Short-acting nitrates help relieve episodes of angina.
Ivabradine added to atenolol improves exercise capacity and relieves angina compared to atenolol plus placebo.
In some studies, ivabradine increased adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

content.Summary: The document discusses clinical guidelines related to refractory angina and chelation therapy. Refractory angina is a challenging condition to manage and often requires consideration of various therapeutic strategies, including enhanced external counterpulsation and revascularization. Chelation therapy involves disodium EDTA and has shown some benefits in specific patient populations with heart disease, although it is not FDA approved for cardiovascular disease treatment.

Refractory Angina.Definition: A chronic chest pain without satisfactory relief from standard medical therapy and not amenable to revascularization.
Refractory Angina.Recommendations: Limited options exist, with enhanced external counterpulsation and transmyocardial laser revascularization being noted but not strongly recommended.

Chelation Therapy.Definition: Therapeutic use of intravenous disodium EDTA to remove heavy metals.
Chelation Therapy.Research Outcomes: TACT trial demonstrated some benefit in patients with previous MI, and ongoing research continues with TACT2.

Revascularization.Overview: Recommendations are being shaped by ongoing research and aim to address comprehensive management of patients with CCD.

Goals of Revascularization: In patients with CCD and lifestyle-limiting angina despite GDMT and significant coronary artery stenoses, revascularization is recommended to improve symptoms.

In patients with CCD with significant left main disease or multivessel disease with severe LV dysfunction (LVEF ≤ 35%), CABG with medical therapy is recommended over medical therapy alone to improve survival.
In patients with CCD and multivessel disease with severe LV dysfunction, CABG added to optimal medical therapy is of intermediate economic value.
In patients with CCD and multivessel CAD appropriate for either CABG or PCI, revascularization in addition to GDMT is reasonable to lower cardiovascular risks.
In selected patients with CCD and significant left main stenosis, PCI can provide equivalent revascularization to CABG.
Decision-Making for Revascularization: For patients with CCD without prior ischemia evaluation, FFR or iFR is recommended before proceeding with PCI.
In patients undergoing coronary angiography without previous stress testing, FFR is recommended before PCI for economic value.
In patients with CCD with complex 3-vessel disease or unclear strategy, a Heart Team approach is recommended.
content.Synopsis: Management of patients with CCD and stable angina focuses on relief of symptoms, prevention of nonfatal events, and improving long-term survival. Revascularization offers greater improvement in angina and QOL. It lowers risks of cardiovascular death, MI, and urgent revascularization. However, the effect on survival is nuanced.

Clinical Trials.Outcomes: Patients experienced worse health status with an invasive strategy compared to a conservative strategy over 36 months of follow-up. Seattle Angina Questionnaire improvements more pronounced with conservative strategy.
PCI objectively reduced ischemia but did not differ in patient-reported freedom from angina compared to placebo.
Patients with lower FFR showed significant QOL improvement at 1 month and 1 year.

Clinical Trials.Findings: Annualized mortality for ACME-2 was 4.0%, 1.0% in FAME-2, and 1.6% in ISCHEMIA.

Clinical Trials.Outcomes: Patients receiving CABG with reduced LVEF had improved survival at 10 years compared to those with medical therapy alone.
PCI does not improve MACEs including mortality with ejection fraction ≤35%.

Cost Effectiveness.Cost Comparison: CABG had a $63,989 per QALY gained compared with medical therapy.

content.Recommendations: Revascularization is advised mainly from older RCTs. Trials focus on CAD with reduced ejection fraction or ischemic cardiomyopathy.

main_points: Revascularization showed no effect on cardiovascular death and MI after four years, but a reduction in mortality was observed with follow-up.
The ISCHEMIA trial noted increased procedural MIs but no significant reduction in overall MI with revascularization.
Spontaneous MI was associated with increased risk of death, while procedural MI showed no such risk.
No direct RCTs compare PCI to medical therapy; focus has been on CABG versus PCI.
Survival rates for PCI and CABG are similar in patients with left main CAD.
FFR-guided PCI in CCD can reduce procedural costs.
FFR in stable angina shows economic value by reducing procedure rates.

trials.name: ISCHEMIA trial
trials.findings: Increased procedural MIs, reduction in mortality with follow-up.

considerations: Patient-centered decisions in Heart Teams are critical in deciding revascularization approaches.

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with CCD who require revascularization for significant left main involvement associated with high-complexity CAD, CABG is recommended in preference to PCI to improve survival.
In patients with CCD who require revascularization for multivessel CAD with complex and diffuse CAD (e.g., SYNTAX score >33), it is reasonable to choose CABG over PCI to improve survival.
In patients with CCD who are appropriate for revascularization but poor candidates for surgery, it is reasonable to choose PCI over CABG to improve symptoms and reduce MACE.

In patients with CCD, diabetes, and multivessel CAD with involvement of the left anterior descending artery who are appropriate candidates for CABG, CABG (with a left internal mammary artery to the left anterior descending artery) is recommended in preference to PCI to reduce mortality and repeat revascularizations.
In patients with CCD and diabetes who have left main stenosis and low- or intermediate-complexity CAD (e.g., SYNTAX score ≤33), PCI may be considered as an alternative to CABG to reduce MACE.

content.synopsis: The document distinguishes the benefits of CABG over PCI in terms of restoring blood flow and reducing long-term risks. CABG is seen to provide protective benefits against ischemic insults and may reduce spontaneous MIs and repeat revascularization. The recommendations are based on studies with predominantly CCD patients, and decisions should involve heart team deliberations and patient-specific factors.

recommendation_specific_support.SYNTAX_trial: The SYNTAX trial showed higher MACE and cardiac mortality for patients with left main and high-complexity disease treated with PCI compared to CABG.

clinical_trials.name: SYNTAX trial
clinical_trials.description: Comparison of PCI with CABG in patients with left main CAD, triple-vessel disease, or both, focusing on patient-level health status outcomes.

clinical_trials.name: OPTIMUM Trial
clinical_trials.description: Focused on patients surgically ineligible for CABG undergoing PCI, having high risks for surgery and complex clinical profiles.

clinical_trials.name: FREEDOM Trial
clinical_trials.description: Compared CABG with PCI in diabetic patients with multivessel disease, highlighting outcomes related to death rates.

key_points: The SYNTAX trial at 10-year follow-up found no significant mortality difference between PCI and CABG for specific conditions.
The OPTIMUM trial showcased complex trials for mentally and physically high-risk surgical patients.
The FREEDOM trial evaluated increased risks for diabetic patients undergoing PCI compared to CABG.
recommendations: Counseling should be provided for patients with CCD who experienced SCAD to recognize triggers.
Evaluate for vasculopathies in patients who experienced SCAD to identify vascular abnormalities.
Beta-blocker therapy may reduce the recurrence of SCAD in applicable patients.

content.SCAD Overview: SCAD is an underrecognized cause of myocardial infarction (MI) that primarily affects women, particularly during periods of physical exertion and stress or during pregnancy. It may also be associated with underlying arteriopathy. Management typically includes conservative measures, with surgical options for unstable patients.
content.Recurrence: Recurrence rates of SCAD vary widely, ranging from 0% to 26.9% at 1 year, with factors such as physical exertion triggering recurrent SCAD.
content.Arteriopathies: SCAD may be related to underlying conditions such as fibromuscular dysplasia and other vascular abnormalities.
content.Screening: The document suggests screening for arteriopathies and connective tissue disorders that may point towards underlying conditions.

content.Summary: This guideline discusses the management of SCAD (Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection) and nonobstructive CAD (Coronary Artery Disease). Beta blockers are associated with a 64% reduction in recurrent SCAD. Statin therapy is not indicated for SCAD unless there are other cardiovascular risks or inherited disorders such as FH (Familial Hypercholesterolemia). For nonobstructive CAD, strategies guided by invasive coronary physiologic testing can improve angina severity and quality of life. Nonobstructive CAD involves myocardial ischemia without obstructive CAD and may be caused by coronary microvascular dysfunction or coronary vasospasm.

recommendations.text: In patients with CCD and cancer, a multidisciplinary team including cardiology and oncology expertise is recommended to improve long-term CVD outcomes.
Women with CCD who are contemplating pregnancy or who are pregnant should be risk-stratified and counseled regarding risks of adverse maternal, obstetric, and fetal outcomes.

Women with CCD who are contemplating pregnancy or who are pregnant should receive care from a multidisciplinary cardio-obstetric care team beginning before conception and continuing throughout pregnancy, delivery, and postpartum to improve maternal and fetal outcomes.
In women with CCD, continuation of statin use during pregnancy may be considered.

synopsis.text: Cancer and CCD share mechanistic pathophysiology and are leading causes of death globally, sharing risk factors like lifestyle, obesity, smoking, diabetes, and age. Patients with CCD and cancer have high cardiovascular risk and are often undertreated. Chemotherapy and radiation therapy commonly used in cancer may have cardiac toxicity and increase CVD risk factors. AHA recommends improved communication between oncology and cardiology specialists and encourages cardio-oncology rehabilitation for patients at risk. Multidisciplinary teams should manage complex cardiovascular adverse effects in cancer treatment, including thromboprophylaxis for high-risk patients.

recommendation_specific_support.text: A multidisciplinary cardio-oncology team should manage patients before, during, and after cancer treatment, focusing on improving cardiovascular outcomes and managing common conditions like thromboembolism prophylaxis. Cardio-oncology is crucial for minimizing negative cardiovascular effects. If no antithrombotic regimen, options like apixaban, rivaroxaban, or low-molecular-weight heparin may be offered.

content.CCD in Pregnancy: CCD is rare among pregnant women, with a prevalence of 2% to 3.6%. Physiologic changes during pregnancy may increase cardiovascular risks, necessitating careful management for women with CCD who become pregnant. This includes heightened maternal and fetal risks and the need for multidisciplinary management.
content.Pharmacologic Therapy: It's important to balance maternal and fetal health. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and other specific drugs should not be used due to potential harm to the fetus.
content.Postmenopausal Hormone Therapy: Women with CCD should avoid postmenopausal hormone therapy due to the lack of benefits and increased risk of venous thromboembolism.
content.Risk Assessment: Women with CCD, especially those with previous SCAD, should undergo thorough evaluation and risk stratification when contemplating pregnancy.
content.Studies and Outcomes: Studies show varying results on cardiovascular complications, with adverse cardiac events reported in specific pregnancies, but current evidence on the impact of hormone replacement therapy on MACE and mortality is lacking.

content.title: Team-Based Cardio-Obstetrics Model of Care

Preconception: Risk assessment: mWHO, CARPREG II, ZAHARA
Labor/Delivery: Monitoring: consider pulse oximetry, telemetry if indicated
Postpartum: Patient counseling on symptoms of complications
Long-Term: Identify women with APO (preeclampsia and hypertensive disorders, gestational diabetes, preterm delivery, small for gestational age)

content.description: The cardio-obstetrics model of care involves multiple specialists working together and with the patient to address issues from preconception, through pregnancy and delivery, and the postpartum period. APO indicates adverse pregnancy outcomes.

content.Statin Use in Pregnancy: A 2014 meta-analysis showed no increase in birth defects due to statin exposure during pregnancy, but found an increased risk of miscarriage attributed to underlying maternal comorbidities. A UK General Practice Research Database study showed a 1.64-fold increased risk of fetal loss with statin-exposed pregnancies. The FDA concluded data are insufficient to determine the risk of miscarriage from statins. Statins are contraindicated during lactation.
content.Lipoprotein Apheresis: Considered for women with heterozygous or homozygous familial hypercholesterolemia and CCD.
content.ACE Inhibitors In Pregnancy: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, direct renin inhibitors cause renal dysgenesis and related issues and are contraindicated. Aldosterone antagonists are also contraindicated due to their anti-androgen effects.
content.Hormone Therapy: No cardiovascular benefit was observed in trials of hormone therapy. The risk of venous thromboembolism was increased in some studies of women with previous events. Combined hormonal contraceptives and medroxyprogesterone acetate should be avoided in women with CCD or thrombosis risk.
content.Older Adults: Patients aged 75 and older have a higher prevalence of CCD. Clinical decisions are complicated by the presence of multiple comorbidities and polypharmacy, making treatment determination difficult. Subgroup analyses suggest medical therapy and revascularization can be effective.

content.increase in cardiovascular events: Associated with significant reduction in major bleeding. Traditional risk scoring systems may need modification for older patients with CCD.
content.older patients: May prioritize maintenance of mobility and functional status over reduced mortality rate. Increase in frailty, malnourishment, and cognitive decline, affecting outcomes and treatment response.
content.treatment options: Team-based care and shared decision-making are crucial. Framework considering 5 Ms of geriatric care may guide discussions.
content.recommendation for CKD: Measures should be taken to minimize risk of treatment-related acute kidney injury in patients with CCD and CKD.
content.CKD and CAD: CKD increases CAD progression risk. Patients on hemodialysis have ~20% higher mortality, ~50% due to cardiovascular causes.
content.postmortem studies: Reveal higher atherosclerosis burden and inflammation evidence in CKD patients.
content.diagnostic testing: Noninvasive testing is often less accurate due to CKD.
content.use of therapy: Limited guidance on pharmacological and interventional therapy due to underrepresentation in trials.
content.ICH presenta: ISHEMIA-CKD trial shows no improved outcomes with revascularization to GDMT in severe ischemia.
content.acute kidney injury: Should not be a reason to withhold PCI in CKD. Risk reduction possible with avoidance of nephrotoxic agents and adequate hydration.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses the management of cardiovascular disease (CVD) in patients with chronic coronary disease (CCD) who also have HIV or autoimmune disorders. It emphasizes antiretroviral therapy for HIV patients and considerations for lipid profiles. For autoimmune disorders, it discusses the use of disease-modifying drugs and biologics.
HIV Recommendations: Antiretroviral therapy lowers the risk of cardiovascular events in patients with CCD and HIV.
Choose antiretroviral therapy regimens favoring cardiovascular and lipid profiles.
Avoid lovastatin or simvastatin with protease inhibitors in CCD and HIV patients.
Autoimmune Disorders Recommendations: Use disease-modifying drugs for rheumatoid arthritis to decrease cardiovascular risks.
Consider biologics and immune modulating therapies to reduce cardiovascular events.
Avoid long-term high-dose glucocorticoids when alternatives are available.
content.Synopsis: HIV and chronic inflammatory conditions increase atherosclerosis and CVD risk. Treatments should consider the lipid profile impact and disease activity control. Multidisciplinary care involving infectious disease and rheumatology expertise is recommended.
