content.topic: Cardiac Allograft Vasculopathy in Heart Transplant Recipients

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with cardiac allograft vasculopathy, statins are recommended for secondary prevention to reduce MACE.
In patients with cardiac allograft vasculopathy, aspirin can be beneficial for secondary prevention to reduce MACE.

In patients with severe cardiac allograft vasculopathy, revascularization is reasonable in those with suitable anatomy to potentially mitigate the adverse long-term consequences of cardiac allograft vasculopathy.

content.synopsis: Cardiac allograft vasculopathy is a leading cause of long-term mortality and retransplantation among heart transplant recipients. It progresses over time, affecting approximately 30% of patients at 5 years and 50% at 10 years post-transplant.
content.diagnosis: Coronary angiography is the accepted clinical standard for screening and diagnosis of cardiac allograft vasculopathy. Intravascular ultrasound is useful to improve detection of angiographically occult cardiac allograft vasculopathy.
content.treatment: Lifestyle modifications and optimal control of cardiovascular risk factors are crucial. Statins, aspirin, and high-intensity interval training are beneficial. CABG is considered in selected patients.
content.supportive_text: RCTs have shown a decreased incidence of cardiac allograft vasculopathy with simvastatin or pravastatin. Statin initiation early post-transplant is associated with significantly lower rates of progression.

recommendations.recommendation: In stable patients with CCD and with previous ACS or coronary revascularization, referral to telehealth programs, community-based programs, or both for lifestyle interventions may be reasonable as an adjunct to usual care to improve management of cardiovascular risk factors.

In patients with CCD without a change in clinical or functional status on optimized GDMT, routine periodic testing with coronary CTA or stress testing with or without imaging is not recommended to guide therapeutic decision-making.
In patients with CCD without a change in clinical or functional status, routine periodic reassessment of LV function is not recommended to guide therapeutic decision-making.
In patients with CCD without a change in clinical or functional status, routine periodic invasive coronary angiography should not be performed to guide therapeutic decision-making.

synopsis.risk: Patients with CCD are at elevated risk for future MACE and should be observed periodically in the outpatient setting.
synopsis.management: Includes long-term risk factor modification and active management of GDMT to achieve maximally tolerated doses, involving effective communication between various care teams.
synopsis.importance: Emphasis on lifestyle and medical interventions, follow-up testing reserved for significant changes in symptoms.

content.trial_details: A nurse-coordinated referral trial with 711 patients led to lifestyle improvements in the Netherlands.
content.prognosis_management_spectrum: Management of asymptomatic patients with CCD on GDMT with functional or anatomic testing isn’t recommended unless symptoms are present.
content.ISCHEMIA_trial: 5,179 patients with CAD were randomized to invasive vs conservative care with no difference in outcomes.
content.CLARIFY_registry: 32,105 outpatients with CCD showed anginal symptoms linked to increased MACE but not silent ischemia.
content.POST-PCI_trial: Routine functional stress testing in high-risk PCI patients showed no benefit at 2 years post-PCI.
content.ReACT_trial: No clinical benefit from routine follow-up coronary angiography versus clinical follow-up alone.
content.asymptomatic_patients_management: Asymptomatic patients did not benefit from invasive management.
content.cost_value_considerations: Discussion of out-of-pocket costs for medications is recommended to prevent nonadherence.

content.Synopsis: The guideline discusses the high out-of-pocket costs of some CCD therapies, which can lead to decreased adoption and increased nonadherence. It emphasizes the importance of clinicians discussing these costs with patients as part of shared decision-making and suggests using lower-cost alternatives and guiding systems to adopt cost-effective therapies.
content.Evidence Gaps: Despite advancements in CCD treatment, gaps remain in noninvasive imaging, risk scores for MACE, patient-reported measures, decision aids, effects of various substances on CCD, and the efficacy of therapies in diverse populations. Further research is needed on personalized medicine and technologies to improve risk assessment.

content.focus: Cardiac Allograft Vasculopathy in Heart Transplant Recipients

sections.heading: 6.9. Cardiac Allograft Vasculopathy in Heart Transplant Recipients
key_points: Various studies and trials are referenced, highlighting the timing and management of cardiac allograft vasculopathy.
Use of hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-coenzyme A reductase inhibitors (statins) is recommended for delaying progression of cardiac allograft vasculopathy.
The impact of immunosuppressive therapies, such as everolimus, sirolimus, and calcineurin inhibitors, is discussed in relation to managing cardiac allograft vasculopathy.
Clinical guidelines suggest regular follow-up and possible conversion to different immunosuppressive agents under specific clinical circumstances.

Assessment through various clinical trials emphasizing personalized treatment and lifestyle interventions.

content.Title: 2023 AHA/ACC/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Chronic Coronary Disease Guideline
Key Points: Focus on chronic coronary disease with emphasis on prevention, lifestyle management, and pharmacologic treatments.
Includes recommendations for managing common comorbidities such as diabetes, hypertension, and obesity.
Attention to cost and access to care, including medication adherence and patient-physician communication about out-of-pocket costs.
Emphasizes shared decision-making and patient engagement in managing heart failure and other related conditions.

context: PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9) inhibitors may be used in select populations for lipid lowering in patients with CCD.

A PCSK9 monoclonal antibody can be beneficial to further reduce the risk of MACE. In patients with CCD who are very high risk, the use of PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies is projected to be of uncertain economic value at US prices.

PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies are mentioned as agents that can improve outcomes when added to statin therapy to further reduce cardiovascular risk, particularly when additional LDL-C lowering is necessary.

The FOURIER trial evaluated evolocumab among those with established ASCVD. The cost-effectiveness of adding generic ezetimibe to maximally tolerated statin therapy is sensitive to the assumption regarding the effect of ezetimibe on cardiovascular and all-cause death.

Clinicians bypassing the addition of ezetimibe before adding a PCSK9 monoclonal antibody should recognize this may not be cost effective. PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies are of low economic value in patients at low to moderate risk of adverse cardiovascular events.
PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies may be considered to further reduce LDL-C levels when ezetimibe and statins are insufficient.

Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA directed to break down PCSK9 mRNA, resulting in reduced synthesis of PCSK9. Inclisiran reduces LDL-C levels by approximately 50% and is administered as a single subcutaneous dose, with a second dose at 3 months, then every 6 months.
